content.problemStatement:
This example shows how to use **anchor tags** (``) to create links that connect to external websites.

---

**When you run the code, you'll see:**

- Three distinct links, all clickable.
- All of these links are **absolute paths**, which means they use a full URL starting with `https://`.
- When you click on any of them, the page will reload and take you to the new site in the same browser tab.

---

**Try it yourself:**

1. Change the `href` attribute on the **"Wikipedia"** link to a different external website, like `https://www.google.com`, to see how easy it is to change the destination.
2. Change the text inside one of the links — for example, change **Google** to **Google Search** and see how the clickable text changes while the destination remains the same.
3. Add another list item and a link to a new external site.

Click **Run** to see how the anchor tag is the crucial element that connects your page to the rest of the web.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
